section.title: The Birth of Samuel
section.sectionRationale: The book opens not in a palace but in the ache of a barren woman. Elkanah has two wives; Peninnah has children and taunts, Hannah has none and weeps. At Shiloh, where a corrupt priesthood is failing, Hannah pours out her soul to the LORD of Hosts and vows that a son given to her will be given back to Him. God remembers her, Samuel is born, and Hannah brings him to the house of the LORD. It is one movement: the LORD raises up a deliverer through the prayer of a lowly, forgotten woman.
section.thesis: When the leadership of Israel is bankrupt and one woman is empty and despised, the LORD who closes and opens the womb hears a prayer poured out in anguish, gives the son who is asked, and receives him back, quietly beginning the rescue of His people through the lowly rather than the powerful.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the barren wife and her rival, verses 1 to 8; the vow poured out at Shiloh, verses 9 to 18; the LORD remembers and Samuel is born, verses 19 to 20; and the child given back to the LORD, verses 21 to 28.

units.label: The barren wife and her rival
units.note: the LORD had closed her womb

verses.text: Now there was a man named Elkanah who was from Ramathaim-zophim in the hill country of Ephraim. He was the son of Jeroham, the son of Elihu, the son of Tohu, the son of Zuph, an Ephraimite.
He had two wives, one named Hannah and the other Peninnah. And Peninnah had children, but Hannah had none.
Year after year Elkanah would go up from his city to worship and sacrifice to the {{tsebaoth|LORD of Hosts}} at Shiloh, where Eli’s two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, were priests to the LORD.
And whenever the day came for Elkanah to present his sacrifice, he would give portions to his wife Peninnah and to all her sons and daughters.
But to Hannah he would give a double portion, for he loved her even though the LORD had closed her womb.
Because the LORD had closed Hannah’s womb, her rival would provoke her viciously to taunt her.
And this went on year after year. Whenever Hannah went up to the house of the LORD, her rival taunted her until she wept and would not eat.
“Hannah, why are you crying?” her husband Elkanah asked. “Why won’t you eat? Why is your heart so grieved? Am I not better to you than ten sons?”

units.label: The vow poured out at Shiloh
units.note: I have poured out my soul before the LORD

verses.text: So after they had finished eating and drinking in Shiloh, Hannah stood up. Now Eli the priest was sitting on a chair by the doorpost of the temple of the LORD.
In her bitter distress, Hannah prayed to the LORD and wept with many tears.

And she made a vow, saying, “O LORD of Hosts, if only You will look upon the affliction of Your maidservant and remember me, not forgetting Your maidservant but giving her a son, then I will dedicate him to the LORD all the days of his life, and no {{nazir|razor}} shall ever touch his head.”
As Hannah kept on praying before the LORD, Eli watched her mouth.
Hannah was praying in her heart, and though her lips were moving, her voice could not be heard. So Eli thought she was drunk
and said to her, “How long will you be drunk? Put away your wine!”
“No, my lord,” Hannah replied. “I am a woman troubled in spirit. I have not had any wine or strong drink, but I have poured out my soul before the LORD.
Do not take your servant for a wicked woman, for all this time I have been praying out of the depth of my anguish and grief.”
“Go in peace,” Eli replied, “and may the God of Israel grant the petition you have asked of Him.”
“May your maidservant find favor with you,” said Hannah. Then she went on her way, and she began to eat, and her face was no longer downcast.

units.label: The LORD remembers and Samuel is born

verses.text: The next morning they got up early to bow in worship before the LORD, and then they returned home to Ramah. And Elkanah had relations with his wife Hannah, and the LORD {{zakar|remembered}} her.
So in the course of time, Hannah conceived and gave birth to a son. She named him Samuel, saying, “Because I have {{shaal|asked}} for him from the LORD.”

units.label: The child given back to the LORD
units.note: he is dedicated to the LORD

verses.text: Then Elkanah and all his house went up to make the annual sacrifice to the LORD and to fulfill his vow,
but Hannah did not go. “After the boy is weaned,” she said to her husband, “I will take him to appear before the LORD and to stay there permanently.”
“Do what you think is best,” her husband Elkanah replied, “and stay here until you have weaned him. Only may the LORD confirm His word.” So Hannah stayed and nursed her son until she had weaned him.

Once she had weaned him, Hannah took the boy with her, along with a three-year-old bull, an ephah of flour, and a skin of wine. Though the boy was still young, she brought him to the house of the LORD at Shiloh.
And when they had slaughtered the bull, they brought the boy to Eli.
“Please, my lord,” said Hannah, “as surely as you live, my lord, I am the woman who stood here beside you praying to the LORD.
I prayed for this boy, and since the LORD has granted me what I asked of Him,
I now dedicate the boy to the LORD. For as long as he lives, he is dedicated to the LORD.” So they worshiped the LORD there.

tsebaoth.gloss: Hosts, armies. The title LORD of Hosts, first used in Scripture here at Shiloh, names the LORD as commander of the armies of heaven and earth. It is fitting that the book which raises up kings and fights the LORD's battles opens by naming Him the true King over every host.

nazir.gloss: One separated or consecrated, a Nazirite. Hannah's vow that no razor will touch her son's head sets him apart to the LORD for life, like Samson before him, Numbers 6. Samuel is given wholly to God from the womb, a life marked out as belonging entirely to the LORD.

zakar.gloss: To remember, call to mind, act on behalf of. When Scripture says the LORD remembered Hannah, it does not mean He had forgotten; it means He moved to act on His covenant care, as He remembered Noah and Israel in Egypt. God's remembering is always the prelude to His saving deed.

shaal.gloss: To ask, request, or lend. It threads through the whole chapter: Hannah asked for a son, the LORD granted what she asked, and she gives him back as one asked of, and lent to, the LORD. The same root stands behind the name Saul, the king Israel will later ask for; here it names a son asked in faith and returned in worship.

basic.context: The book of 1 Samuel opens in a dark time. The judges have failed, the priesthood at Shiloh is corrupt, and there is no king. Into that emptiness comes a barren, grieving woman named Hannah, tormented by her husband's other wife. Year after year she goes up to worship and comes home in tears. Then at Shiloh she pours out her soul to the LORD of Hosts and vows that if He gives her a son, she will give him back to God for life. God remembers her, Samuel is born, and Hannah keeps her word and brings the little boy to live in God's house. The question underneath it all is: where will Israel's rescue come from? The answer, surprisingly, is through the prayer of a lowly, forgotten woman.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, telling us what actually happened, not simply giving us a model to copy. Hannah is not rewarded because she bargained well; the point is that God delights to lift up the lowly and to work His salvation through the weak and overlooked. We should not turn her vow into a formula for getting what we want from God. The abiding truths are that God hears the brokenhearted, that He opens and closes the womb, and that He is quietly beginning to raise up a deliverer when Israel is at its lowest.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter opens by naming the LORD of Hosts, tsebaoth, the commander of heaven's armies, the true King over Israel. Hannah vows her son as a nazir, one set apart to God, no razor touching his head. The turning point is that the LORD zakar, remembered, Hannah, His remembering always moving Him to act. And the whole story turns on the word shaal, to ask: Hannah asked for a son, God granted her request, and she gives him back as one asked of and lent to the LORD.
basic.typology: Hannah's song of a womb opened by God foreshadows another miraculous birth announced to a lowly woman, Mary, whose song echoes Hannah's own. Samuel, given wholly to God from the womb to serve in the house of the LORD, points ahead to the true Prophet and Priest who does always the will of His Father. And a barren woman given a son by God's power is a recurring sign that salvation is His gift, not human achievement.
basic.christ: The God who opens the barren womb and raises up a deliverer from an obscure hill town is the same God who, in the fullness of time, would send His Son through a lowly virgin in an obscure town called Bethlehem. Hannah's prayer, and the song that follows it in chapter 2, sets the key for the whole story of salvation: God brings down the proud and lifts up the humble. Samuel, the asked-for son given back to God, dimly points to the Son whom the Father gives and who gives Himself back in perfect obedience.
basic.god: This chapter shows a God who is not distant from the tears of the overlooked. He is the LORD of Hosts, sovereign over armies, yet He bends to hear one weeping woman. He is the God who opens and closes the womb, who remembers, and who gives. And He is beginning, quietly and gently, to answer the chaos of the age of the judges, not with a mighty man but with a praying woman and a little boy.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 30 and other barren-mother stories show God giving life where there was none. Luke 1 records Mary's song, which deliberately echoes Hannah's. Psalm 113 sings of the God who lifts the needy and gives the barren woman a home. And 1 Peter 5 promises that God gives grace to the humble.
basic.oneStory: At the lowest point of the judges, God begins Israel's rescue through the prayer of a barren woman. The son He gives, Samuel, will anoint the kings through whom the promise runs, and the pattern set here, God lifting the lowly and giving the asked-for son, runs straight through the story to Bethlehem, where God gives His own Son to save His people.

context.literary: The opening of 1 Samuel and the hinge from the age of the judges. The narrator sets a scene of family sorrow and a failing sanctuary, then draws all eyes to one praying woman. Her vow and God's answer set up the song of chapter 2, which announces the book's great theme, and introduce Samuel, the prophet who will anoint Israel's first two kings.
context.historical: The setting is Shiloh, the central sanctuary where the ark and tabernacle rested before the temple in Jerusalem. Barrenness carried deep social shame and left a woman vulnerable, especially in a household with a rival wife. Elkanah's yearly pilgrimage and sacrifices reflect ordinary faithful worship in a period when, as the last verses of Judges say, everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
context.canonical: This chapter begins the transition from judges to monarchy that shapes the rest of 1 and 2 Samuel. The theme of God raising the lowly, sounded here and sung in chapter 2, becomes a thread that runs to Mary's Magnificat and to the gospel itself. The Nazirite dedication links Samuel back to Samson and marks him as wholly the LORD's.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Israel's rescue, when its leaders had failed, began not with the powerful but with the prayer of a lowly, despised woman whom God heard and answered, and to introduce Samuel, the God-given prophet through whom the LORD would raise up king and kingdom.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Hannah's grief, vow, and God's gift; it does not prescribe vows as a technique to obtain children or turn her bargain into a formula. What endures is that God hears the brokenhearted, sovereignly gives life, and delights to work salvation through the humble.

debates.question: Was Hannah's vow an attempt to bargain with God, or an act of surrendered faith?

views.label: A costly act of faith and surrender
views.case: Hannah asks for a son not to possess him but to give him back to the LORD for life; the vow's very shape, the longed-for gift returned to God, marks it as worship and surrender rather than a bargain struck for her own benefit.

views.label: A vow within ordinary Israelite piety
views.case: Vows were a recognized and permitted part of Old Testament worship, Numbers 30; Hannah is not manipulating God but bringing a heartfelt petition in the accepted form of her day, and God honors the faith beneath it.

debates.bearing: Either way the emphasis falls on God, who hears and gives, and on Hannah's readiness to give her dearest treasure back to Him; her prayer models honest, surrendered petition, not a transaction that obligates God.

debates.question: Why does the narrator dwell on Hannah rather than on the leaders of Israel?

views.label: To announce the book's theme of reversal
views.case: By opening with a barren, taunted woman rather than priest or judge, the narrator signals that God will raise up the lowly and bypass the proud, the very theme Hannah will sing in chapter 2 and that governs the whole book.

views.label: To contrast true faith with a failing sanctuary
views.case: Eli and his sons represent a priesthood in decline; Hannah's earnest, humble prayer shines against that failure, showing where real faith is found while official religion falters.

debates.bearing: Both readings prepare us to see that God's saving work advances through unexpected, humble people, and to look for the King He will raise up, ultimately Christ, born of a lowly woman.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's language keeps pointing beyond Hannah to her God. The scene is set at the shrine of the tsebaoth, the LORD of Hosts, the commander of heaven's armies, a title used here for the first time in Scripture; the God who fights Israel's battles opens the book by hearing one woman weep. Hannah vows her son as a nazir, separated to the LORD, no razor ever touching his head, so that the child belongs wholly to God from the start. At the pivot the narrator says the LORD zakar, remembered, Hannah, a word that never means God had forgotten but always that He now moves to act in covenant faithfulness, as He remembered Noah and His people in Egypt. And over the whole chapter hangs the verb shaal, to ask: Hannah asked for a son, the LORD granted what she asked, and she gives him back as one asked of, and lent to, the LORD. That same root will name Saul, the king Israel later demands; here it names a son asked in faith and joyfully returned in worship.

typology.type: The barren womb opened by God to give a deliverer
typology.fulfillment: A recurring sign that salvation is God's gift, reaching its climax when God gives His Son through a virgin, Luke 1:26-38.

typology.type: Hannah's prayer of reversal, the lowly lifted up
typology.fulfillment: Mary's Magnificat, which echoes Hannah and celebrates the God who scatters the proud and exalts the humble, Luke 1:46-55.

typology.type: Samuel, given wholly to God from the womb to serve in His house
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Prophet and Priest set apart to do always the Father's will, Hebrews 10:5-10.

study.christ: The God who opens a closed womb and raises a deliverer from an obscure hill town is the God who, in the fullness of time, sent His Son through a lowly woman in an obscure town, Galatians 4:4. Hannah's story sets the key that the gospel will sing: God brings down the proud and lifts up the humble, the theme her own song announces in chapter 2 and Mary takes up in Luke 1. Samuel is the asked-for son given back to God, a faint shadow of the beloved Son whom the Father gives and who gives Himself back in perfect obedience for us. Where Hannah longed for a child to end her reproach, God gives His Son to end ours, and to make the barren rejoice.
study.god: This chapter reveals a God who is high and near at once. He is the LORD of Hosts, sovereign over the armies of heaven, yet He bends low to hear one weeping, forgotten woman. He is the God who closes and opens the womb, so that life is His gift and never a human achievement. He remembers His people, and His remembering moves Him to act. And in a bleak age, when priests are corrupt and no king reigns, He is already at work, beginning Israel's rescue not through the mighty but through a humble prayer and a little boy set apart to serve Him.

crossReferences.note: Mary's Magnificat deliberately echoes Hannah's song of God lifting the lowly and casting down the proud.
God remembered Rachel and opened her womb, one of several barren mothers through whom He gave the child of promise.
The LORD raises the poor from the dust and settles the barren woman as a joyful mother, the very reversal Hannah experiences.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, who in due time He exalts, the pattern set in Hannah's prayer.

study.oneStory: At the lowest ebb of the judges, with the priesthood corrupt and no king in Israel, God begins the rescue of His people through the prayer of a barren, despised woman. The son He gives, Samuel, will grow to anoint the kings through whom the covenant promise runs toward David and, at last, toward great David's greater Son. The pattern set here, God remembering the forgotten, lifting the lowly, and giving the asked-for son, runs straight through Scripture to Bethlehem, where God gives His own Son to save His people.

quiz.q: Why does the narrator open the book of 1 Samuel with Hannah rather than with a priest or judge?

options.text: To announce the book's theme that God lifts up the lowly and works His rescue through the humble and overlooked.
options.note: Hannah embodies the reversal she will sing in chapter 2, the key to the whole book.

options.text: To show that the priesthood at Shiloh was failing while true faith was found elsewhere.
options.note: True and part of the contrast, but the deeper point is the theme of God exalting the lowly.

options.text: Because Hannah was the most powerful person in Israel at the time.
options.note: The opposite is true; she was lowly and despised, which is precisely the point.

options.text: Because the birth of Samuel has nothing to do with the rest of the book.
options.note: Samuel is central; he will anoint Israel's first kings.

quiz.q: What does it mean that the LORD remembered Hannah?

options.text: God moved to act in covenant faithfulness on her behalf, giving her the son she asked for.
options.note: In Scripture God's remembering is the prelude to His saving action, as with Noah and Israel.

options.text: God gave her a son after she had prayed and vowed at Shiloh.
options.note: True in outcome, but understates that remembering means God acting in covenant care, not simply responding to the vow.

options.text: God had briefly forgotten Hannah and then recalled her situation.
options.note: God had not forgotten; remembering here is covenant action, not the ending of forgetfulness.

options.text: God rewarded Hannah because her bargain obligated Him to act.
options.note: The gift is God's gracious act, not a debt owed to a successful bargain.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Hannah's vow that no razor would touch her son's head?

options.text: She was setting Samuel apart to the LORD as a Nazirite, wholly consecrated to God for life.
options.note: The unshaven head marks the Nazirite vow, Numbers 6; Samuel belongs entirely to God.

options.text: She was promising to give her son to serve at the house of the LORD.
options.note: True in effect, but the specific sign named is the lifelong Nazirite consecration.

options.text: She was following a custom that had nothing to do with dedication to God.
options.note: It was precisely a sign of dedication, not an empty custom.

options.text: She was ensuring her son would become a wealthy and powerful man.
options.note: The vow gives him to God's service, not to worldly power.

quiz.q: How does Hannah's prayer and her son point forward in the story of salvation?

options.text: God giving a son to a lowly woman, and her song of reversal, anticipate Mary and the birth of Christ.
options.note: Hannah's song is echoed in Mary's Magnificat; God still lifts the lowly and gives the promised Son.

options.text: They show that God sometimes answers the prayers of those who are sad.
options.note: True but thin; the passage points to God's larger pattern of salvation through the humble.

options.text: They teach that anyone who makes a vow will receive whatever they ask.
options.note: This misreads the vow as a formula; the emphasis is on God's gracious giving.

options.text: They have no connection to the coming of Christ.
options.note: The birth of a deliverer to a lowly woman clearly foreshadows the gospel.

quiz.q: What does this chapter reveal about God?

options.text: He is the LORD of Hosts, sovereign over all, yet He bends low to hear one forgotten, weeping woman.
options.note: The chapter holds together God's majesty and His tender nearness to the lowly.

options.text: He is a God who opens and closes the womb, so that life is His gift.
options.note: True and important, but only part of the fuller picture of His majesty joined to His nearness.

options.text: He is a distant ruler who only concerns Himself with kings and armies.
options.note: The chapter shows the opposite; the LORD of Hosts hears one grieving woman.

options.text: He is unable to help the barren or the brokenhearted.
options.note: He is precisely the One who gives life and lifts the lowly.

questions: Hannah brought her deepest grief straight to the LORD and poured out her soul before Him. Where do you need to stop carrying your sorrow alone and bring it honestly to God?
Peninnah's taunts drove Hannah to prayer rather than away from God. How can the very things that provoke and wound you become the occasion to seek the LORD more earnestly?
Hannah asked for a son and then gave him back to God. What is the treasure you are most tempted to grasp, and what would it look like to hold it before God with open hands?
The LORD remembered Hannah and acted. How does it steady you to know that God's remembering His people is always the beginning of His saving work, even when the answer is slow?
God began Israel's rescue through a lowly, overlooked woman. Where are you tempted to think God only works through the impressive and powerful, and how does this chapter correct that?

translationNotes.note: Ramathaim-zophim is a place name; the BSB footnote notes it may read from Ramathaim, a Zuphite, comparing the Septuagint and 1 Chronicles 6.
A double portion follows the BSB reading; the footnote allows a choice portion, marking Elkanah's special love for Hannah either way.
The name Samuel sounds like the Hebrew for heard of God; Hannah links it to her asking, since the same root, shaal, means to ask and to lend or dedicate.
Some Dead Sea Scrolls add that Hannah would offer him as a Nazirite for all time, making explicit the lifelong consecration implied in her vow of verse 11.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 1:1-28. Prose narrative. Hyphens appear only within proper names such as Ramathaim-zophim; no em or en dashes anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
